research_summary: Comprehensive enrichment research on 5 potential foundation partners conducted via web search and direct website access. Focus on grant opportunities, application procedures, and fit assessment for patient safety mission.
research_notes: Two foundations (Helen Diller, Ford) explicitly do not accept unsolicited proposals per their public policies. Three family foundations have minimal online presence and limited public grant information. Phone numbers verified for all foundations.

location.address: 1 N B St, Suite 4000, San Mateo, CA 94401

foundations.assets_last_known: Large endowment ($1.1B+ committed to UCSF alone)

current_opportunities.name: Diller Teen Tikkun Olam Awards
current_opportunities.description: Recognizes Jewish teens nationwide for leadership and social impact work
current_opportunities.focus: Teen leadership, Jewish causes, social impact
current_opportunities.eligibility: Jewish teens only - NOT relevant to PSMF organizational funding

current_opportunities.name: Diller Educator Awards
current_opportunities.eligibility: Educators in San Francisco Bay Area - NOT relevant to PSMF

how_to_apply.requirements: Foundation does NOT accept unsolicited grant applications from organizations
how_to_apply.notes: The Helen Diller Foundation and Helen Diller Family Foundation support specific programs and partnerships only. Unsolicited proposals are explicitly rejected on their website. Only institutional partnership approaches (e.g., through UCSF) are considered.

deadlines.notes: Only Diller Teen Tikkun Olam Awards has public deadline. No general grant deadlines as unsolicited proposals not accepted.

current_priorities: Health and Medical Research (especially cancer research at UCSF)
Jewish Causes and Global Jewish Needs

contact.secondary_contact: Jennifer Smith (Executive Director of Foundation Programs)

notes: CRITICAL BARRIER: Foundation policy explicitly rejects unsolicited proposals
No public application process or portal exists
Healthcare focus is primarily on cancer research at UCSF, not patient safety broadly
Grant sizes are very large ($500M+) suggesting they fund major research institutions only
Relationship-driven grantmaking focused on UCSF partnership
Would require established institutional intermediary to even be considered
red_flags: Explicit rejection statement on website: Does not accept unsolicited proposals
Closed grantmaking process with no public application portal
Extremely large grant sizes indicate not interested in typical nonprofit requests
Strong UCSF bias suggests grantmaking is relationship-driven for insiders
No evidence of interest in healthcare quality/safety vs. basic science research
Located in San Mateo, but grantmaking focused on UCSF partnerships
strengths: Location in California enables potential relationship building
Healthcare is within foundation's scope (though limited to medical research, especially cancer)
fit_assessment.recommendation: NOT RECOMMENDED for direct outreach. Foundation policy explicitly rejects unsolicited proposals. PSMF would require an established relationship with UCSF or similar major research institution to be considered. Direct application would violate stated policy.

foundations.data_quality: High - Direct website access and content verification

current_opportunities.name: BUILD Initiative (Invitation Only)
current_opportunities.description: $2B signatory commitment to strengthen social justice movements and institutions
current_opportunities.deadline: N/A - Invitation only to current Ford grantees
current_opportunities.focus: Social justice institutions, equity movements, structural change
current_opportunities.eligibility: Current Ford grantees only - CLOSED to new unsolicited applicants

how_to_apply.requirements: Ford Foundation explicitly states: Does NOT accept unsolicited proposals by mail, email, or phone. Applications only through website portal for limited open opportunities.
how_to_apply.notes: Ford's grantmaking model is proactive, not reactive. Program teams identify promising organizations and work WITH them to develop proposals. Applicants must be 'well-known and respected' with strong media coverage and impact reports to get on their radar.

deadlines.notes: Most Ford opportunities are by invitation only. Open opportunities rotate annually. NYC Good Neighbor expected reopening late Summer/Fall 2025. JustFilms deadlines vary.

current_priorities: Reproductive Justice and Healthcare Access (bodily autonomy, abortion access in ban states)
Worker Health Policies (paid sick leave, family leave, home care quality)

healthcare_focus.summary: Limited and Indirect - NOT patient safety focused
healthcare_focus.details: Ford funds healthcare-related work primarily through: (1) Reproductive justice initiatives (bodily autonomy, abortion access in states with bans), (2) Worker health policies (paid sick days, paid family leave, improving home care quality), (3) Long-term care policy innovation. Does NOT fund direct patient safety programs, medical research, or clinical quality improvement initiatives.
healthcare_focus.fit_with_psmf: Weak - Patient safety does not align with Ford's stated healthcare priorities

contact.email: Not listed for general grant inquiries
contact.program_officer: Multiple program teams by issue area (not publicly listed by individual)
contact.strategy_note: Foundation suggests networking with previous Ford grantees and building media presence as way to get 'on their radar'

recent_focus_2024_2025: Reproductive justice and healthcare access in abortion-ban states
Worker health and labor standards globally
Racial justice and ethnic equity initiatives
funding_history.grantee_profile: Social justice-focused organizations, civic engagement nonprofits, racial equity leaders, reproductive rights advocates, environmental organizations. NOT biomedical/clinical organizations.
funding_history.typical_grant_range: Large multi-year grants, though amounts vary by program

notes: CRITICAL BARRIER: Does NOT accept unsolicited proposals per stated policy
Healthcare funding limited to reproductive justice, labor, long-term care - explicitly NOT patient safety
PSMF mission (clinical patient safety) fundamentally misaligned with Ford's social justice focus
Would require exceptional reputation and significant media presence to get on their radar
BUILD initiative (Ford's largest funding mechanism with $2B) is invitation-only to existing grantees
Recent leadership changes may further restrict already-narrow opportunities
<1% success rate for unsolicited applications indicates extremely low probability
red_flags: Explicitly rejects unsolicited proposals on official website
Healthcare priorities don't include clinical quality or patient safety
<1% success rate for unsolicited applications
Grantmaking is proactive/invitation-based, not responsive to written applications
New foundation president pledged to 'retool' grantmaking - potential further restrictions
Rumor in philanthropy sector: BUILD program ($2B commitment) may be discontinued
Fraud warning on website about unauthorized individuals impersonating foundation
strengths: Very large endowment ($16B) provides significant funding capacity
Some healthcare engagement (though not patient safety aligned)
Open to national organizations (not geographically limited)
fit_assessment.recommendation: NOT RECOMMENDED for direct outreach. Ford Foundation's mission and grantmaking process fundamentally misaligned with PSMF patient safety focus. <2% estimated probability given unsolicited proposal policy, misaligned healthcare priorities, and <1% historical success rate.

how_to_apply.notes: Small family foundation with $870K in assets. Leadership (Kumar Bhavnani, Dilip Bhavnani) dedicate 10 hours per week. No documented public grant application process. Grantmaking appears to be informal and discretionary.

deadlines.notes: No published deadlines available. Foundation likely operates on rolling basis or by direct contact with family leadership.

current_priorities: No specified focus areas listed in public documents
Past grantees located in: Closter NJ, Hagerstown MD, Los Angeles CA
Geographic coverage: California, Maryland, New Jersey

notes: Very small foundation ($870K assets) with limited annual capacity (~$177K)
Average grant size of $28K suggests PSMF requests would exceed typical size by 3-4x
No specified funding priorities makes fit assessment uncertain and risky
Foundation is family-operated with informal process - no clear guidelines

red_flags: Very small asset base ($870K) with limited annual giving capacity (~$177K)
No public website or published grant guidelines
No specified funding priorities or focus areas
Minimal online presence suggests either dormancy or very informal operation
Average grant size (~$28K) is significantly below typical institutional nonprofit funding needs

Informal family foundation structure suggests relationship-based, not merit-based giving
strengths: Located in California (same state as PSMF)
Appears to accept applications (unlike Helen Diller or Ford)

potential_fit_scenarios: If foundation has undocumented healthcare or safety interests (possible given limited transparency)
For smaller pilot projects or capacity-building grants under $50K
Through personal networks or relationships with Kumar or Dilip Bhavnani
If Bhavnani family has philanthropic interests in patient safety not yet documented publicly
fit_assessment.recommendation: POSSIBLE but LIMITED. Small asset base and unclear priorities suggest low-to-moderate probability. Worth investigating further if PSMF has California nonprofit networks or connections that know this family foundation. Direct phone outreach (818-623-9898) to ask about funding interests and application process recommended. Assess fit only if foundation confirms healthcare/safety interest. Not priority target unless connection exists or foundation articulates relevant mission.

fit_assessment.recommendation: NOT RECOMMENDED. Extremely small grant sizes ($18.3K average), geographic restrictions to specific cities, unclear priorities, and minimal information suggest this is not viable funding source for PSMF. Foundation appears designed to give modest grants to specific California communities known to the Robertson family. Direct outreach unlikely to be successful unless PSMF operates specifically in Lodi, Santa Barbara, Stockton, or Woodbridge AND foundation confirms healthcare interest.

foundations.assets_characterization: Significantly larger than average California nonprofits

current_opportunities.description: Foundation has documented focus on medical field with specific interest in pulmonary/pulmonic conditions. Supports schools, colleges, and cultural organizations.

how_to_apply.notes: Foundation operates through US Bank as custodian/administrative contact. Suggests older-style foundation with traditional bank trust structure. No online application portal or published guidelines available.

deadlines.notes: Available records indicate 'NONE LIMITED TO MEDICAL FIELD' - suggests no specific deadlines but funding restricted to medical field and pulmonary conditions.

current_priorities: Schools and cultural organizations (per IRS activity codes)

No website or published grant guidelines
Specific medical focus (pulmonic conditions) may exclude patient safety work unless directly clinical
Administrative structure through bank suggests formal, institutional giving patterns
No evidence of interest in healthcare quality/safety rather than condition-specific treatment
33 years of operation but no online presence suggests dormancy or minimal public engagement
Foundation appears very traditional/formal in operations
strengths: Explicit medical field focus aligns with PSMF healthcare mission
Located in San Francisco - California-based enables relationship development
Significantly larger than average foundation assets suggest funding capacity

potential_fit_scenarios: If PSMF has work in pulmonary/respiratory patient safety (highly specific but relevant alignment)
If PSMF partners with pulmonary/respiratory care centers for safety initiatives
For general medical safety initiatives if foundation interprets 'medical field' broadly
If PSMF can frame patient safety as part of pulmonary medicine advancement
fit_assessment.recommendation: POSSIBLE with CAUTION - HIGHEST PRIORITY OF FAMILY FOUNDATIONS. Foundation has explicit medical field focus which is more aligned with PSMF than most other targets. However, lack of public information, specific pulmonic focus, and unclear grant process present challenges. STRONGLY RECOMMEND: (1) Phone contact through US Bank NA (858-410-0986) to inquire about current grant interests, application process, and whether patient safety/quality improvement fits within foundation scope, (2) Request and review recent 990-PF filings to understand recent grantee patterns and infer true priorities, (3) Assess whether PSMF can articulate specific pulmonary/respiratory patient safety work. Probability 25-40% is highest among family foundations but still uncertain due to limited transparency. This is the only realistic target among the three family foundations.

foundations.data_quality: Low-Moderate - Minimal public information. Primarily from nonprofit databases. No website or published materials.

key_takeaways: CRITICAL: 2 of 5 foundations (Helen Diller, Ford) explicitly do NOT accept unsolicited proposals. Both have public policy statements rejecting unsolicited applications.
Of the 3 family foundations with potential openness: 2 are extremely small ($870K-$896K assets) with very limited funding capacity (avg grants $18K-$28K)
Only 1 foundation (Swanton) has documented medical/healthcare focus, though transparency is minimal
None of the 5 foundations have explicitly stated patient safety as a funding priority
Overall finding: Limited landscape for PSMF among this set of 5 foundations. Unsolicited approach not viable for 2 of 5 (40%). Small family foundations (3 of 5) have limited capacity.
Recommended action: Focus outreach on Harold Swanton Foundation only, with caution

viable_targets.foundation: Harold R Swanton and Winifred Swanton Foundation
viable_targets.reason: Only foundation with documented medical field focus. California-based. Limited transparency requires direct inquiry via US Bank NA (858-410-0986). Specific pulmonary focus requires fit assessment.
viable_targets.recommended_action: Phone outreach to US Bank NA to: (1) Confirm foundation still active, (2) Inquire about healthcare quality/safety fit, (3) Request application process and recent grantee list, (4) Assess if PSMF's work aligns with pulmonary/respiratory care focus

not_viable.reason: Explicitly rejects unsolicited proposals. Closed grantmaking process. No application portal.
not_viable.recommended_action: NOT RECOMMENDED for direct outreach. Only viable if PSMF can partner with UCSF or similar major research institution.

not_viable.reason: Explicitly rejects unsolicited proposals. Healthcare priorities (reproductive justice, worker health, long-term care) misaligned with patient safety. <1% historical success rate for unsolicited applications.

marginal_targets.reason: Very small ($870K assets), unclear priorities, no website, average $28K grants. Would require specific California network connection.
marginal_targets.recommended_action: OPTIONAL - Phone outreach (818-623-9898) only if PSMF has California nonprofit network connection or relationship with Bhavnani family.

marginal_targets.reason: Extremely small ($896K assets), geographic restrictions to specific cities (Lodi, Santa Barbara, Stockton, Woodbridge), only 6 grants total awarded, average $18.3K grants.
marginal_targets.recommended_action: NOT RECOMMENDED unless PSMF operates specifically in one of the four target California cities AND has existing network connection.

research_challenges: Multiple foundations lack websites or published grant guidelines (Bhavnani, Robertson, Swanton)
Patient safety as a specific funding focus not documented for any of the 5 targets
Large foundations (Helen Diller, Ford) have explicitly closed processes rejecting unsolicited proposals
Small family foundations lack transparency in decision-making and actual funding priorities
Limited ability to assess true funding interests without direct foundation contact
Swanton Foundation operates only through bank contact, making information gathering difficult
research_recommendations: PRIORITY: Phone outreach to Harold R Swanton and Winifred Swanton Foundation through US Bank NA (858-410-0986). Ask: (1) Is foundation still active? (2) What is current application process? (3) Does healthcare quality/safety fit within medical field focus? (4) Can you send recent 990-PF and list of recent grantees?
SECONDARY: Request recent 990-PF filings for all 3 family foundations to assess grantee patterns, geographic distribution, and infer true priorities
TERTIARY: Assess whether PSMF has existing relationships or connections in California nonprofit networks that might know Bhavnani or Robertson family foundations
NOT RECOMMENDED: Pursue Helen Diller or Ford Foundation without institutional intermediaries (e.g., UCSF partnership for Diller)

research_metadata.research_completeness: High for large foundations (Diller, Ford); Low-Moderate for family foundations
research_metadata.confidence_level_diller: High - Website access confirmed policy on unsolicited proposals
research_metadata.confidence_level_ford: High - Website access confirmed policy on unsolicited proposals
research_metadata.research_method: Systematic web search, direct website access, nonprofit database research
